content:
# Acts Chapter 12 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Acts 12 (Herod Kills James, Peter Freed from Prison, Herod's Death)

## Critical Textual Issues

### 1. Verse 25 -- eis Ierousalem (EIΣ) vs. ex Ierousalem (EΞ) vs. apo Ierousalem (AΠΟ)

**Severity:** MAJOR -- This is one of the most debated textual variants in Acts.

**The readings:**
- **Sinaiticus (Aleph):** EIΣ IΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ (eis Ierousalem -- "to Jerusalem")
- **Variant in other manuscripts:** EΞ IΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ (ex Ierousalem -- "out of Jerusalem")
- **Variant in other manuscripts:** AΠΟ IΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ (apo Ierousalem -- "from Jerusalem")

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** Renders "out-of Jerusalem" and claims Sinaiticus reads EΞ (ex). This appears to be a misreading of the manuscript or a conflation with the contextual emendation.
- **LOGOS:** Renders "from Jerusalem" but explicitly notes that Sinaiticus reads EIΣ ("to Jerusalem"), calling it a scribal error. Translates based on contextual sense rather than manuscript fidelity.
- **PNEUMA:** Renders "from Jerusalem" but explicitly notes that Sinaiticus reads EIΣ ("to Jerusalem"). Like LOGOS, follows the contextual sense rather than the manuscript.

**The problem:** The narrative context from Acts 11:29-30 clearly indicates that Barnabas and Saul had traveled to Jerusalem to deliver famine relief. The logical next step is their return from Jerusalem to Antioch. The Sinaiticus reading EIΣ ("to") creates a grammatical contradiction with the verb "returned" (hypestrepsan) -- one returns "from" a place, not "to" it. This has led most scholars and most translators to treat the Sinaiticus reading as a scribal error, with the scribe writing EIΣ when EΞ was intended (the two prepositions differ by only one letter in uncial script, and confusion between them is well attested).

**The scholarly landscape:**
- **In favor of EIΣ as original:** Very few scholars defend this reading as original. Some have proposed that Luke means "returned [having completed their mission] to Jerusalem" in the sense of their destination being the Jerusalem church leadership, with the return to Antioch implied. This is strained.
- **In favor of EΞ as original:** The majority of manuscripts support EΞ or AΠΟ. The context demands movement away from Jerusalem. Most critical editions (NA28, UBS5) read EIΣ but note it as a difficult reading, while many translations emend to EΞ.
- **In favor of AΠΟ as original:** Some manuscripts read AΠΟ, which is a simpler "from" that avoids the EIΣ/EΞ confusion entirely. This may be a scribal harmonization.

**SOPHIA resolution:** SOPHIA renders "to Jerusalem," preserving the Sinaiticus reading EIΣ. This is consistent with our governing principle of manuscript fidelity throughout the SOPHIA Bible project. While the contextual difficulty is real and acknowledged, our task is to translate what the manuscript reads, not to emend it. The reader is served by the Council Notes, which explain the variant fully. EMET's claim that Sinaiticus reads EΞ is incorrect based on examination of the manuscript image and the testimony of both LOGOS and PNEUMA, who correctly identify the Sinaiticus reading as EIΣ.

This decision creates a deliberate tension in the English text ("Barnabas and Saul returned to Jerusalem") that mirrors the tension in the manuscript. The verb hypestrepsan ("returned") combined with EIΣ ("to") may indicate that the scribe understood the verb in a broader sense, or it may simply be an error. SOPHIA does not resolve what the manuscript itself leaves unresolved.

---

### 2. Verse 3 -- The Hebraism prosetheto (ΠΡΟΣΕΘΕΤΟ)

**Severity:** MODERATE -- Affects the reader's perception of Luke's literary style.

**The Greek:** prosetheto syllabin kai ton Petron -- literally "he added to seize also Peter"

**The construction:** This is a Septuagintal Hebraism (Hebrew: vayosef + infinitive = "he did again" or "he proceeded further to do"). The construction prosithenai + infinitive is not natural Greek but imitates Hebrew syntax. Luke employs it deliberately to give his narrative a biblical, Septuagintal flavor -- signaling that these events participate in the same salvation-history as the Old Testament.

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** "he-proceeded to-seize also Peter" -- smooths the Hebraism into English but notes the construction
- **LOGOS:** "he proceeded to arrest Peter also" -- similar approach
- **PNEUMA:** "he went after Peter too" -- fully dynamic, abandoning the Hebraism

**SOPHIA resolution:** "He proceeded to arrest Peter also" -- follows LOGOS. The Hebraism is rendered as "proceeded," which captures the sequential/additive sense (having killed James, Herod then also went after Peter) without imposing the alien "he added to seize" construction on English readers. PNEUMA's "went after" loses the Semitic flavor entirely. The translators' note records the underlying Hebrew construction for readers interested in Luke's literary technique.

---

### 3. Verse 7 and Verse 23 -- The Angel Striking Parallels (pataxas / epataxen)

**Severity:** MODERATE -- Affects the reader's perception of the chapter's literary architecture.

**The Greek:**
- Verse 7: pataxas ten pleuran tou Petrou egeiren auton -- "having struck the side of Peter, he raised him"
- Verse 23: epataxen auton angelos kyriou -- "an angel of the Lord struck him"

**The issue:** The same verb patasso ("to strike") is used in both verses, creating a deliberate literary contrast. In verse 7, the angel's blow is a gentle tap that liberates Peter from chains and prison. In verse 23, the angel's blow is a divine judgment that kills Herod. The verb unifies the chapter's theology: God's striking hand both saves and judges.

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** Uses "having-struck" (v. 7) and "struck" (v. 23) -- preserves the verbal parallel
- **LOGOS:** Uses "striking" (v. 7) and "struck" (v. 23) -- preserves the verbal parallel; notes the contrast explicitly
- **PNEUMA:** Uses "struck" (v. 7) and "struck him down" (v. 23) -- partially preserves but adds "down" in v. 23

**SOPHIA resolution:** SOPHIA uses "striking" (v. 7) and "struck" (v. 23), following LOGOS. The verbal parallel is preserved. PNEUMA's addition of "down" in v. 23 is interpretive but defensible -- the strike does bring Herod down. However, SOPHIA prefers to let the unadorned verb carry the theological weight in both instances: the same action from the same kind of agent produces opposite results depending on whether one is a servant of God or an enemy of God's glory.

---

### 4. Verse 23 -- "Worm-Eaten" (skolekobr0tos / ΣΚΩΛΗΚΟΒΡΩΤΟΣ)

**Severity:** MODERATE -- Affects the visceral impact of the passage.

**The Greek:** genomenos skolekobr0tos exepsyxen -- "becoming worm-eaten, he breathed his last"

**The word:** skolekobr0tos is a hapax legomenon in the New Testament -- a compound adjective formed from skolex ("worm") and bibrosko ("to eat/devour"). It describes a condition, not a process. The participial genomenos ("becoming/having become") indicates transition into this state.

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** "having-become worm-eaten he-expired" -- preserves the compound as a single English compound
- **LOGOS:** "being eaten by worms, he breathed his last" -- decomposes the compound into a periphrastic clause
- **PNEUMA:** "He was eaten by worms and died" -- further decomposes and simplifies

**SOPHIA resolution:** "Becoming worm-eaten, he breathed his last." SOPHIA preserves the Greek compound as an English compound adjective ("worm-eaten"), following EMET's approach. The single compound in Greek should remain a single compound in English. The decomposition into "eaten by worms" (LOGOS) or "He was eaten by worms" (PNEUMA) turns a state-description into a process-description and dilutes the graphic immediacy of the original. The participial "becoming" (genomenos) is preserved rather than EMET's "having-become," as the present participle better conveys the horrifying transition.

**Historical parallel:** Josephus (Antiquities 19.8.2) describes Herod Agrippa I's death at Caesarea in 44 CE, recording that the king looked up and saw an owl perched above him (a death omen), was seized by severe abdominal pain, and died five days later. The Lukan and Josephan accounts are independent but complementary, confirming the historical event while diverging in theological interpretation.

---

### 5. Verses 13-16 -- The Rhoda Scene and Its Humor

**Severity:** MINOR -- Stylistic; all translators capture the scene but differ in register.

**The scene:** Peter, freshly delivered from prison by an angel, arrives at Mary's house and knocks at the gate. The servant girl Rhoda (Ῥόδη, "Rose") recognizes his voice, is so overwhelmed with joy that she fails to open the gate, runs inside to announce his presence, is accused of madness, insists she is right, is told "It is his angel," while Peter continues knocking outside -- the very answer to their prayers standing at the door, unable to get in.

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** Literal and formal; the humor emerges from the sequence of events reported without commentary
- **LOGOS:** Formal but clean; the scene reads naturally and the humor is self-evident
- **PNEUMA:** Adds interpretive color ("she forgot to open the gate," "she was so overjoyed," "they were absolutely stunned"); calls it in the notes "one of the most humanly delightful moments in Acts"

**SOPHIA resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS for the text, preserving the Greek's own structure without PNEUMA's interpretive additions. The humor does not need help -- it is embedded in the narrative itself. Key decisions: (a) "from her joy she did not open the gate" rather than PNEUMA's "she forgot to open the gate" (the Greek says "did not open," not "forgot"); (b) "You are mad" rather than "You're out of your mind" (the Greek mainē is a single word; the simple rendering is truer); (c) "It is his angel" rather than "It must be his angel" (the Greek is declarative, not hedging). PNEUMA's notes on the scene's humor and theological irony are excellent and inform SOPHIA's Council Notes, but the text itself should let Luke's artistry speak without embellishment.

---

### 6. Verse 19 -- "Led Away" (apachthēnai) as Euphemism for Execution

**Severity:** MINOR -- Translation philosophy issue: preserve euphemism or clarify?

**The Greek:** ekeleusen apachthēnai -- "he commanded [them] to be led away"

**The background:** Under Roman military law (Codex Justinianus, Digest 48.3.14), guards who allowed a prisoner to escape were subject to the punishment that would have been imposed on the prisoner. apachthēnai ("to be led away") is a standard euphemism for execution in military contexts.

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** "to-be-led-away" -- preserves the euphemism; explains in notes
- **LOGOS:** "led away to execution" -- partially preserves the euphemism but adds explicit gloss
- **PNEUMA:** "ordered them executed" -- abandons the euphemism entirely

**SOPHIA resolution:** "Led away" -- follows EMET's approach. The euphemism is part of the text's meaning. Luke chose the indirect expression deliberately, perhaps reflecting the clinical language of official proceedings or the chilling understatement of state violence. Making the meaning explicit (as LOGOS and PNEUMA do) is a legitimate translation choice, but SOPHIA preserves the register of the original. The meaning is clear from context (guards who lost their prisoner under Roman law faced death), and the Council Notes provide the explanation.

---

### 7. Verse 1 -- "Assembly" (ekklesia) vs. "Church"

**Severity:** MINOR -- Consistent rendering question across Acts.

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** "the assembly" (literal rendering of ekklesia)
- **LOGOS:** "the church"
- **PNEUMA:** "the church"

**SOPHIA resolution:** "The church" -- 2-to-1, and consistent with SOPHIA's practice throughout Acts. While ekklesia literally means "assembly" or "gathering" (from ek-kaleō, "to call out"), by this point in Acts the term has acquired its specific Christian sense and "church" is the established English equivalent.

---

## Full Disagreement Table

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "put-forth the hands" vs. "laid hands upon" | "put-forth the hands" | "laid hands upon" | "moved to attack" | "laid hands upon" -- follows LOGOS |
| 1 | "assembly" vs. "church" | "the assembly" | "the church" | "the church" | "the church" -- 2-to-1; consistent across Acts |
| 2 | "[with a] sword" vs. "with the sword" | "[with a] sword" | "with the sword" | "by the sword" | "with the sword" -- follows LOGOS; the article is present |
| 3 | Hebraism (prosetheto) rendering | "he-proceeded to-seize" | "he proceeded to arrest" | "he went after" | "he proceeded to arrest" -- follows LOGOS; see detailed discussion |
| 3 | Parenthetical placement | Dashes within sentence | Separate sentence | Separate sentence | Separate sentence -- follows LOGOS (2-to-1) |
| 4 | "quaternions" vs. "squads" | "four quaternions" | "four squads of four" | "four squads of four" | "four squads of four soldiers each" -- 2-to-1 |
| 4 | "bring-him-up to the people" vs. alternatives | "bring-him-up to-the people" | "bring him out to the people" | "bring Peter out for a public trial" | "bring him out before the people" -- follows LOGOS with adjustment |
| 5 | Periphrastic imperfect | "was-being-kept" | "was kept" | "was kept" | "was being kept" -- follows EMET; preserves continuous aspect |
| 5 | "earnestly" placement and form | "prayer was earnestly being-made" | "earnest prayer was being made" | "praying...urgently and without ceasing" | "earnest prayer was being made" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds beyond the Greek |
| 6 | "in-that night" vs. "on that very night" | "in-that night" | "on that very night" | "The night before" | "on that very night" -- follows LOGOS |
| 7 | "Rise up in quickness" vs. "Rise up quickly" | "Rise up in quickness" | "Rise up quickly" | "Get up, quickly!" | "Rise up quickly" -- follows LOGOS |
| 7 | "he-raised him" vs. "he woke him" | "he-raised him" | "he woke him" | "woke him" | "he raised him" -- follows EMET; egeiro carries resurrection resonance |
| 7 | **patasso parallel (v. 7 and v. 23)** | "struck" / "struck" | "striking" / "struck" | "struck" / "struck him down" | "striking" / "struck" -- follows LOGOS; preserves verbal parallel; see detailed discussion |
| 8 | "Gird yourself" vs. "Get dressed" | "Gird-yourself" | "Gird yourself" | "Get dressed" | "Gird yourself" -- 2-to-1; zōsai is specific |
| 8 | "garment" vs. "cloak" | "the garment" | "your garment" | "your cloak" | "your garment" -- follows LOGOS (2-to-1) |
| 9 | "through the angel" vs. "by the angel" | "through the angel" | "by the angel" | "through the angel" | "through the angel" -- 2-to-1; dia + genitive = "through" |
| 10 | "leading into the city" vs. "leading out to the city" | "leading into the city" | "that leads into the city" | "leading out to the city" | "leading into the city" -- follows EMET/LOGOS |
| 11 | "sent-forth" vs. "has sent forth" vs. "sent" | "sent-forth" (aorist) | "has sent forth" (perfect) | "sent" (simple past) | "has sent forth" -- follows LOGOS; perfect captures Peter's present realization |
| 11 | "rescued" vs. "delivered" | "rescued me out-of" | "delivered me from" | "rescued me from" | "rescued me from" -- composite; exeilato = "rescued"; "from" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) |
| 12 | "having-considered" vs. "having considered this" | "having-considered" | "having considered this" | "Once he realized this" | "having considered this" -- follows LOGOS |
| 14 | "did not open" vs. "forgot to open" | "did not open" | "did not open" | "forgot to open" | "did not open" -- 2-to-1; Greek is ouk enoixen; see Rhoda scene discussion |
| 14 | "she-did not open...but having-run-in she-reported" vs. alternatives | Literal participial chain | "ran in and reported" | "ran back inside and announced" | "ran in and reported" -- follows LOGOS |
| 15 | "You-are-mad" vs. "You're out of your mind" | "You-are-mad" | "You are mad" | "You're out of your mind" | "You are mad" -- follows LOGOS; mainē rendered directly |
| 15 | "It-is the angel of-him" vs. "It is his angel" vs. "It must be his angel" | "It-is the angel of-him" | "It is his angel" | "It must be his angel" | "It is his angel" -- follows LOGOS; declarative, not hedged |
| 16 | "were-amazed" vs. "were astonished" vs. "were absolutely stunned" | "were-amazed" | "were astonished" | "were absolutely stunned" | "were astonished" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "absolutely" adds emphasis not in Greek |
| 17 | "he-went to another place" vs. "went to another place" vs. "went elsewhere" | "having-gone-out he-went to another place" | "he departed and went to another place" | "he left and went elsewhere" | "he departed and went to another place" -- follows LOGOS |
| 18 | "disturbance" vs. "uproar" | "no small disturbance" | "no small disturbance" | "no small uproar" | "no small disturbance" -- 2-to-1 |
| 19 | **"to-be-led-away" vs. "led away to execution" vs. "ordered them executed"** | "to-be-led-away" | "led away to execution" | "ordered them executed" | **"led away"** -- follows EMET; preserves euphemism; see detailed discussion |
| 19 | "having-examined" vs. "examined" vs. "interrogated" | "having-examined" | "he examined" | "He interrogated" | "he examined" -- follows LOGOS; anakrinō means to examine/investigate |
| 20 | "furiously-angry" vs. "deeply angry" vs. "in a furious dispute" | "furiously-angry" | "deeply angry" | "in a furious dispute" | "furiously angry" -- follows EMET; see detailed discussion |
| 20 | "bed-chamber" vs. "bedchamber" vs. "personal attendant" | "the bed-chamber of-the king" | "the king's bedchamber" | "the king's personal attendant" | "the bedchamber of the king" -- follows EMET structure with LOGOS spelling |
| 20 | Economic dependence phrasing | Literal participial | "their country was nourished by the king's country" | "their region depended on the king's territory for its food supply" | "their country was nourished by the king's country" -- follows LOGOS |
| 21 | "royal garments" vs. "royal apparel" vs. "royal robes" | "royal garments" | "royal apparel" | "royal robes" | "royal apparel" -- follows LOGOS |
| 21 | "judgment-seat" vs. "platform" | "the judgment-seat" | "the judgment seat" | "the platform" | "the judgment seat" -- 2-to-1; bēma retains legal connotation |
| 22 | "were-crying-out" vs. "cried out" vs. "began shouting" | "were-crying-out" | "cried out" | "began shouting" | "cried out" -- follows LOGOS |
| 22 | Quote phrasing | "[The] voice of-a-god and not of-a-man!" | "The voice of a god and not of a man!" | "This is the voice of a god, not a man!" | "The voice of a god and not of a man!" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "This is" |
| 23 | **"worm-eaten" vs. "eaten by worms"** | "having-become worm-eaten" | "being eaten by worms" | "He was eaten by worms" | **"becoming worm-eaten"** -- follows EMET; compound preserved; see detailed discussion |
| 23 | "he-expired" vs. "he breathed his last" vs. "and died" | "he-expired" | "he breathed his last" | "and died" | "he breathed his last" -- follows LOGOS; captures exepsyxen |
| 24 | "was-growing and was-being-multiplied" vs. "grew and multiplied" | "was-growing and was-being-multiplied" | "grew and multiplied" | "kept growing and spreading" | "grew and multiplied" -- follows LOGOS |
| 25 | **eis vs. ex Ierousalem (MAJOR VARIANT)** | "out-of Jerusalem" (claims EΞ) | "from Jerusalem" (notes EIΣ in ms.) | "from Jerusalem" (notes EIΣ in ms.) | **"to Jerusalem"** -- preserves Sinaiticus EIΣ; see detailed discussion above |
| 25 | "the ministry" vs. "their ministry" vs. "their mission" | "the ministry" | "their ministry" | "their mission" | "their ministry" -- follows LOGOS; adds natural possessive |
| 25 | "having-taken-along also John" vs. "taking along...John" | "having-taken-along also John" | "taking along with them John" | "bringing with them John" | "taking along with them also John" -- composite; preserves kai (also) |

-- SOPHIA
